title: Build Version Two
developmental_frame: At 11–12, children can handle the real iteration loop: get a thing into several people's hands, gather feedback that's actually useful, decide what matters, and build a genuine second version. This is a clear step up from Q1's single-user test — the leap from 'I made a thing' to 'I made it noticeably better because I listened'. It also introduces a mature judgement: not every request should be built.
authentic_value_meaning: Value here is making something measurably better by listening to real users — the core loop every real builder lives by. It's the difference between a one-off project and a thing that actually improves, and the pride is in the visible jump from v1 to v2.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child watches several real people use their creation, then builds a version two that's clearly better — and gets to see users prefer it. The satisfaction of a real, visible improvement driven by their own good decisions is deeply motivating, and it feels like how 'real' makers and companies work.

artifact: Version two of a digital or physical thing, improved from feedback gathered from at least three real users, with a short before/after showing what changed and why.
success_criteria: At least three real people used version one, and the child gathered their feedback.
The child chose which changes mattered instead of trying to do all of them.
A real version two exists, and the child can explain why each change was made.

lessons.title: Get it into real hands
lessons.concept: One tester tells you a little; several tell you a pattern. Get your thing (from Q1, or a new small one) into the hands of at least three real people and let them actually use it — no hovering, no explaining.
lessons.real_example: A quiz app tested by one friend seems fine. Tested by five, you notice three of them get stuck on the same screen — that's a real pattern you'd never have seen from one.
lessons.apply_task: Get your thing used by at least three real people. Just watch and let them use it naturally.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Did more testers reveal problems one wouldn't have? Patterns need a few people to show up.
lessons.feedback_task: Ask each tester to simply use it while you watch quietly. Note where each one struggles.
lessons.teach_back_task: Explain to someone why three testers beat one for finding real problems.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Real human testers find real human confusion — something AI can't simulate well. Use real people here; no shortcut.
success_criteria: At least three real people used the thing while the child observed.

lessons.title: Gather feedback that's useful
lessons.concept: Vague feedback ('it's nice') is useless. Ask questions that get real answers: 'What was confusing?' 'What would you change?' 'Where did you get stuck?' And trust what you *saw* them do over what they politely say.
lessons.real_example: A tester says 'it's great!' but you watched them fail the same button three times. What they *did* is the real feedback; the polite words are just being kind.
lessons.apply_task: For each tester, write down one thing they said and one thing you saw them struggle with.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Where did what people said differ from what they did? Which do you trust more, and why?
lessons.feedback_task: Compare your notes with a parent — do any struggles show up across multiple testers?
lessons.teach_back_task: Show a friend how to ask feedback questions that get useful answers, not just 'it's nice'.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: AI can help you write good feedback questions to ask. But the feedback itself must come from real users doing real things — that's the data that counts.
success_criteria: Useful feedback (said and observed) is captured for each tester.

lessons.title: Decide what to change
lessons.concept: You can't and shouldn't build every request. Pick the changes that matter most — the problems several people hit, or the one that ruins the experience. Saying no to some feedback is as important as saying yes to the rest.
lessons.real_example: Testers asked for rainbow colours, a login, and a fix for the stuck button. The stuck button broke the whole thing for three people — that's the one that matters. The rainbow can wait forever.
lessons.apply_task: List all the feedback, then circle the two or three changes that matter most. Note why you're skipping the rest.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Was it hard to ignore some requests? Good builders choose; they don't just obey every suggestion.
lessons.feedback_task: Explain your chosen changes to a mentor and why you're skipping the others. Does your reasoning hold?
lessons.teach_back_task: Show someone why building every request would actually make a thing worse.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: This choosing is judgement — the most human part of building. Don't outsource it; a list of 'what users want' isn't a decision about what to build.
success_criteria: Two or three priority changes are chosen with reasons for skipping others.

lessons.title: Build v2 and compare
lessons.concept: Build your version two with the changes that mattered. Then show the before and after — ideally to a tester — so the improvement is visible. Seeing v1 and v2 side by side is proof your listening paid off.
lessons.real_example: V2 fixes the stuck button and adds clearer instructions. You show a tester both versions and they breeze through v2. That visible jump is the whole reward.
lessons.apply_task: Build version two with your priority changes. Write a short before/after: what changed and why.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Is v2 clearly better? What did listening to real users get you that guessing wouldn't have?
lessons.feedback_task: Show v2 to at least one original tester. Ask if it's better and where.
lessons.teach_back_task: Walk someone through your v1 → v2 story and how feedback drove it.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: AI can help you build the changes, but keep the rule from Q1: understand every part. A v2 full of parts you can't explain isn't really your improvement.
success_criteria: A real version two exists with the priority changes built.
The child can explain each change and show the improvement.
